tone: Primarily lighthearted and fun — dorm life, rivalries, chaos, friendship, competition — with genuine dramatic stakes that escalate through each tier. The comedy and the tragedy earn each other. Season 1 ends with the class intact but the world more complicated than it was.

tiers.title: Welcome to U.A.
tiers.overall_tone: Almost entirely fun. This is orientation. The class is meeting each other, figuring out who they are, discovering the school is hard and also ridiculous. Three villain factions exist in Murano — none of them are talking to the class yet.
tiers.dm_at_a_glance: Your job in Tier 1: establish the class as a cohort, not a score bracket. Run the danger lightly. The three faction seeds (Chain, Null Signal, Fracture) are background detail — findable if players look, not announced to anyone. If players chase a thread, give them one more piece. If they don't, those seeds are there for retrospective recognition in Tier 2.

arcs.name: The Entrance Exam
arcs.dm_goal: Drop the class into chaos before they have a plan. The cooperation instinct — or lack of it — is the data Oshiro wants. Who these people are under pressure is the data you want.
arcs.description: A staged disaster scenario in a simulated city block. Students must rescue civilians (actors and prop mannequins) while combat drones provide the villain opposition. No predetermined groupings — everyone is on their own. This is how the class meets each other.
beats: Opening chaos: the exam starts without warning — no countdown, no announcement. Students scatter and self-organise in real time. The first interaction most PCs have is nearly colliding with each other.

Key moments: Toshida shouts a word and accidentally levels a wall. Nakaya enlarges a bus to use as a ramp, then can't unshrink it. Ren disappears mid-sprint — no one sees him move, they just notice he's somewhere else.
Conflict point: Masahata loudly criticises everyone else's technique during the exam. He is correct about roughly half of it, which makes it significantly worse.
Drone boss: the final obstacle is a single large combat drone targeting the highest-scoring candidates. Students have to cooperate — or use each other as distractions — to take it down.
Resolution: the class passes collectively. Score rankings are posted. Masahata reads them twice. The ranking chart era begins.

villain_seeds.fracture: Graffiti on the arena's exterior wall during student arrival: 'HEROES SERVE COMMISSIONS, NOT COMMUNITIES' in the jagged-line Fracture style. Weathered enough to have been there for weeks. Nobody on the exam staff comments on it.
villain_seeds.null_signal: A compact black drone with no UA markings logged the exam from the upper observation tier. The exam team assumed it was another faculty member's equipment. It was not in the manifest. No footage was flagged.
villain_seeds.the_chain: Absent from direct play. A single offhand comment from an agency worker at the venue gates: 'Avoid the port district after dark this week. It's been interesting lately.' Nobody asks a follow-up question.

arcs.name: Dorm Life & Combat Training
arcs.type: Social arc + first formal training encounter
arcs.dm_goal: Establish who these students are to each other before anything is on the line. The dorm incidents build personality. Capture the Flag is where rivalries get specific. Both should feel low-stakes and character-driven.
arcs.description: The class moves into Heights Alliance. First week is pure character chaos — personalities collide, routines form, the common room becomes a social ecosystem. Oshiro's opening combat class (Capture the Flag) runs in the second session: two teams, live stakes, no mercy. The same students who argued about the couch are now trying to beat each other for lap-running rights.
beats: The doorframe incident (Toshida, day one): a word escapes him mid-yawn. A panel falls out of the doorframe. He apologises and fixes it. The panel falls again the next morning.
The couch incident (Nakaya, day two): she absentmindedly enlarges a throw pillow. It cannot be un-enlarged. The couch is now partially obscured. This is simply the couch now.

Masahata prints and laminates a ranking chart based on exam scores. It appears on the fridge, fully formatted with tier brackets. No one asked him to do this. Kinosho stares at it for a long time and says nothing.

Capture the Flag — Oshiro's opening combat class: team assignments calibrated for discomfort, not balance. Yoshibara immediately doubles herself — Oshiro rules it legal because he didn't say it wasn't. Kinosho correctly predicts the outcome of three matchups and declines to share until after. Ando loses to someone he was certain he'd beat and wears his sunglasses for the next 36 hours including during sleep.
Masahata updates the ranking chart to include 'combat performance (preliminary).' No methodology is explained. It is accurate.

villain_seeds.fracture: A leaflet appears under a dorm door on day three. Black print, spare design, jagged-line symbol. 'Your quirk is yours — not your license.' Someone pushed it through. Nobody saw who. Nobody has seen this symbol before (unless they noticed the entrance exam graffiti — if they did, they'll notice it matches).
villain_seeds.null_signal: If any student explores Hamagawa's open-door office in the evening, a monitor in standby shows a file header before the screensaver kicks in: 'Provisional License Cohort Data — External Access Request, Ref: SA/7-91.' Requesting party: 'Seika Advanced Systems.' The file is already closed when they look.
villain_seeds.the_chain: The common room TV runs news in the background one evening. Port district. Something about a business closure — a small support agency for unlicensed quirk users, closed 'due to changing neighbourhood conditions.' The report is twelve seconds long.

arcs.name: The Agency Visit
arcs.dm_goal: First time in the real world without exam conditions. The Chain becomes tangible. All three faction seeds converge in the same physical location for the first time — findable by observant players, not announced.
arcs.description: The class assists at Patchwork Agency in the port district for a day. Filing, crowd management at a public event, basic patrol. Their first taste of actual hero work. Mostly administrative and slightly boring. A minor incident near the end brings the class's first real Chain encounter — and if students are paying attention, two other organisations are also present in the margins.
beats: Arrival: the agency is underwhelmed by having twenty students in their breakroom simultaneously. They did not fully plan for this.
Aikawa organises an entire filing system the agency didn't ask for. It is objectively better than what they had. They are mildly resentful.
Crowd management at a small community event. Toshida is stationed away from the speakers. This is communicated very clearly. He is stationed near the speakers anyway due to a logistical mix-up.

The incident: two Chain Grunts and a Chain Hardskin are intimidating a shopkeeper in a side street. The class has six seconds before Patchwork arrives. Decision point: wait or intervene. Both choices are defensible. The Hardskin's fear when contained — not of the students, but of being seen by them — is the most important data in the encounter.

Patchwork's debrief: direct, accurate, slightly alarming. She's been watching since they arrived. 'The quiet one — that's not local. You walked into someone's business trip.' She won't say more today. She makes a quiet call after they leave.

villain_seeds.the_chain: The Hardskin has a chain-link tattoo visible on DC 15 Perception during the fight, DC 12 on a subdued target. His fear of being identified is disproportionate for a low-level criminal caught by student heroes. That gap is the important data. Patchwork says 'affiliated' once and doesn't elaborate.
villain_seeds.null_signal: Ryo Karashi ('Splice') appears in a corridor inside Patchwork Agency. Visitor badge: 'Seika Consulting — Dr. R. Karashi.' Slim tablet, unhurried, claims wrong floor. If stopped: warm, apologetic, gives nothing away. The tablet, if glimpsed, shows a student photo grid — the class's entrance exam publicity shots. He is gone in thirty seconds.
villain_seeds.fracture: The Fracture jagged-line symbol is scratched into the concrete of the alley wall behind where the Chain Hardskin was standing. Old enough to be weathered — months, not weeks. Not a message placed for today. A marker. Someone was using this alley before the Chain was.

tiers.villain_presence: All three factions are background seeds in Tier 1. The Chain is the most visible — one direct encounter with low-tier operatives, planted through a pro hero's offhand comment. Null Signal is glimpsed twice: the unmarked exam drone, Splice's thirty-second corridor appearance. Fracture is found rather than encountered: graffiti, a leaflet, a symbol scratched in concrete. None of them have spoken to the class yet. All three are aware of them.

tiers.title: Getting Good
tiers.overall_tone: The Sports Festival is peak chaos and excitement — the class gets to show off and it is almost entirely fun. Then the world gets complicated: Fracture becomes a real moral problem the class can't punch away, and the Convergence finale puts all three factions in the same warehouse at the same time. Season 1 ends with a win that doesn't feel entirely clean.
tiers.dm_at_a_glance: Three arcs, each with a different tone. Sports Festival: pure energy, use the full bracket below, every fight has a story. Fracture Problem: moral complexity, Shard should land real arguments. Convergence: three-faction chaos, Kuma is the boss fight, Splice is the complication, Undertow is the image Season 1 ends on. The class wins the immediate problem. Everything else stays open.

arcs.name: The Sports Festival
arcs.dm_goal: Pure excitement. Almost entirely fun. Every fight should have a story. PC fights are fully played out. NPC fights get one-paragraph summaries read aloud. The crowd, the commentary, and the post-fight moments are as important as the results.
arcs.description: U.A.'s annual Sports Festival. Public, broadcast, massive audience. Full tournament structure: obstacle race → cavalry battle → individual brackets with Class 1-B competing alongside Class 1-A. The bracket is detailed below — run it as a living event, not a cutscene. The 1-A vs 1-B rivalry becomes concrete here for the first time.
beats: Pre-festival: the class arrives to a stadium already full. The broadcast team is interviewing hero course students outside. Kazama is recognised from the entrance exam footage. He loses his composure for approximately four seconds.

Obstacle race: Takazuki wins her heat (nobody can find her to pass her). Ren places first overall (nobody saw him pass anyone — he was just suddenly at the front). Setaka's iron balls ricochet through a bottleneck and eliminate four competitors simultaneously. Kinosho places top five despite appearing to be in a near-sleep state for most of the course. Morida Takeshi (Class 1-B) places third — first time the class sees him in a competitive context.

Cavalry battle: alliances form and collapse. Class 1-B's coordination is visibly superior — they've drilled for this. Yoshibara doubles the wrong person at the wrong moment (an opponent already in the lead). This becomes a permanent campaign reference.

Broadcast: the commentary team is unprepared for approximately half the quirks on display. 'What Is She Doing?' about Kinosho trends nationally within twenty minutes of first airing. Masahata's ongoing score commentary in the waiting room is captured on a spectator's phone and briefly goes viral.
Post-festival: the ranking chart — which Masahata brought to the festival in laminated form — does not get updated for four days. When asked why: 'The model is broken.' He reprints it anyway.

event_1_obstacle_race.summary: All participants. Mixed classes competing. Results determine seedings for individual brackets.
key_moments: Takazuki wins her heat — invisible the entire race; nobody knew she was competing until she crossed the line
Ren places 1st overall — nobody saw him pass anyone; he was simply at the front
Setaka's iron balls ricochet through a midpoint bottleneck and eliminate four competitors at once
Kinosho places top 5 despite appearing to be in a near-sleep state for a significant portion of the course
Morida Takeshi (1-B) places 3rd — first meaningful rival appearance in a competitive context; the class has had his entrance exam score on their fridge chart since week one

event_2_cavalry_battle.summary: Teams of 4. Collect headbands. Top 16 overall advance to individual brackets.
key_moments: Yoshibara doubles herself mid-battle — then doubles the wrong person (an opponent already in the lead). Her double and the opponent's lead-holder collide in the arena centre. The scoring board updates in the class's favour for thirty seconds before correcting.
Class 1-B's team coordination is surgical. They've drilled cavalry formations specifically. They make no redundant movements. It's slightly alarming to watch.
Kinosho's team doesn't appear to be coordinating. They place 3rd. Nobody can explain how.

event_3_individual_tournament.format: 16 competitors, mixed from Class 1-A and 1-B. Top 4 seeded — all competitors play from Round 1; seeds receive favorable matchups. Single elimination.

seeds.name: Ren Suzuki
seeds.note: Placed 1st in obstacle race. The class has been watching him move. Nobody knows yet what his speed looks like in a direct fight.

seeds.name: Morida Takeshi
seeds.note: Placed 3rd in obstacle race. Class 1-A has had his entrance exam score on the ranking chart since week one. He is quiet about this. He has been thinking about it too.

seeds.name: Chosen Tadachi
seeds.note: Placed well across both events. Notable for the quality of his obstacle race finish despite Stress not being a movement quirk.

seeds.name: Ogiwara Bunta
seeds.note: Seismic shockwaves. The class felt them in the cavalry battle from across the arena. He was in a different quadrant.

round_1.how_it_goes: Hellflame draws the first massive crowd reaction of the day. Too much reaction — Kazama loses composure managing it. He wins anyway, but he's rattled and the crowd knows it. His exit from the arena is fast.
round_1.dm_note: Play the crowd noise as a genuine obstacle for Kazama. He can't control that he's visually spectacular. If this is a PC fight, have spectators start cheering mid-move — forcing him to choose between commitment and crowd management.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Kazama's critical moment hesitation — the crowd roar hitting right as he commits — costs him. He pulls the blast a fraction. Takuma walks through the reduced discharge and lands the deciding hit. The commentary team goes quiet. The crowd does not know how to react to Hellflame losing.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Takuma Rei advances to QF-3 instead of Kazama. Chosen faces hardening rather than Hellflame escalation — he wins QF-3 but at lower escalation than the default, meaning he's calmer going into SF-2.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Kazama sitting in the stands for the rest of the tournament is a significant character beat. He has to watch. He doesn't leave. Masahata quietly removes the 'crowd factor: asset' notation from his chart.

round_1.how_it_goes: Toshida's Comic shout disrupts Toragi's first Twin Impact — the timing breaks. She absorbs the chaos and shuts him down methodically on the second attempt. The crowd goes silent for three seconds when the Impact lands, then extremely loud.
round_1.dm_note: Let Toshida's disruption feel like a genuine near-upset. The first Impact failing should surprise people. Toragi winning the second is inevitable — but it shouldn't feel like it in the moment.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Toshida's timing on the second disruption is better than anyone expected — including Toragi. She has two Impact approaches drilled. He breaks both. She has no third approach ready. Toshida wins by surviving until she runs out of prepared responses. He looks surprised. She looks like she's already cataloguing what went wrong.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Toshida advances to QF-2 vs Morida. Morida's deflection operates by feel, not rhythm — sound disruption doesn't interfere with it. Morida wins QF-2 cleanly. SF-1 changes: Morida faces whoever won QF-1 instead of Toragi. The Morida/Toragi post-fight conversation doesn't happen.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Toragi watching Morida defeat Toshida in QF-2 is worth a moment. She sees Morida handle the same disruption that beat her without any difficulty. She writes something down. It's about the third Impact she doesn't have yet.

round_1.how_it_goes: Kinosho deactivates Aikawa's iron claws mid-swing. Then walks up to her. The fight ends in approximately forty seconds. The audience does not understand what happened. 'What Is She Doing?' — segment one — trends nationally within the hour.
round_1.dm_note: Don't explain Kinosho's win to the audience. The commentary team can't explain it either. The class should be slightly disturbed. Masahata should look at the chart, say nothing, and sit down.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Kinosho's Neutralization requires a targeting window — a moment where she can identify the right limb and close. Aikawa's iron claws don't give her that window. The swings are too fast, too varied. Aikawa lands the hit Kinosho didn't expect because Kinosho's first plan assumed she'd get the window. She didn't. The genuine upset the crowd was expecting actually happens. 'What Is She Doing?' never airs.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Aikawa advances to QF-4 vs Ogiwara. Seismic shockwaves don't care about iron claws — the ground she's standing on isn't stable enough for her to drive the strikes. Ogiwara wins. SF-2 changes: Ogiwara faces Chosen or Kazama instead of Kinosho.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Kinosho sitting in the stands is very quiet. She watches every subsequent fight. She is not visibly upset. She is watching something specific in each fight and nobody knows what.

round_1.how_it_goes: Ando's scales shrug off the first engine-charge cleanly. Izuto clocks this and adjusts — times his second burst for the moment Ando is mid-pivot repositioning. Ando goes down surprised. He wears the sunglasses for the rest of the day.
round_1.dm_note: Ando's first shrug should feel definitive — the class should believe he's going to win. Izuto's adjustment is the key character beat: he's a problem-solver under pressure.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Izuto's second burst hits when Ando is already braced for it — he read the adjustment coming. He planted and shrugged the second charge the same way he shrugged the first. Izuto ran out of adjustments. Ando wins by being less complicated than Izuto expected. He takes the sunglasses off for a moment, looks at them, puts them back on.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Ando advances to QF-1 vs Ren. Scales can't block something you can't track — Ren wins, but it's less clean than the Izuto fight. The third-place match changes if Ren advances: Ando vs whoever lost the other SF.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Izuto watching Ren beat Ando in QF-1 is worth playing. He's watching the QF-1 fight like he's studying it. He has opinions about Ando's footing in round 2. He doesn't say them out loud.

round_1.how_it_goes: Takazuki disappears before the opening bell. Ren wins in eleven seconds. He crossed the arena faster than the commentary team could finish the introduction. Takazuki's strategy assumed her opponent needed to find her first. Ren didn't look. The crowd replays the footage three times trying to find where the contact happened.
round_1.dm_note: Run this as proof-of-concept for Ren's tier. The fight shouldn't feel like a fight — it should feel like a demonstration. The class watching should clock that Takazuki had a real plan and it simply didn't matter.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: She was invisible before the bell and stayed that way. Ren's eleven-second plan assumed she'd be somewhere specific when he arrived. She wasn't. He overshot. In the half-second he was reorienting, she landed the hit. Nobody saw it. The crowd replays the footage eight times. Nobody is certain what happened on the footage either. The commentary team goes very quiet for a different reason than usual.
alternate_outcome.downstream: MAJOR UPSET — Ren is eliminated in Round 1. Takazuki advances to QF-1 vs Izuto (or Ando if alt R1-4). Izuto figures out her displacement pattern through air current readings — problem-solver finds the solution. Izuto wins QF-1. SF-1 completely changes: Izuto vs whoever won QF-2. Ren watching the tournament from the stands is its own story — he doesn't leave, he watches every fight, he is thinking about what a second attempt would look like.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: This is the biggest possible upset of the tournament. If it happens, let it breathe. The class's reaction to Ren losing in Round 1 is more significant than the fight itself. Masahata does not update the chart. He stares at it for a long time.

round_1.how_it_goes: Yoshibara doubles herself immediately — standard opening. Morida deflects the first attack from the double without hesitation. He explains afterward, without being asked: 'Different weight. Clean tell.' She tries four more doubles. He deflects each one correctly. She stops doubling and attempts a direct hit. He deflects that too. First definitive 1-B win of the individual tournament. The crowd doesn't understand why none of her tricks worked. Masahata takes extremely detailed notes.
round_1.dm_note: Play Yoshibara as genuinely trying to adapt — she's not being stupid, Morida is just reading her perfectly. His win should feel technically impressive in a quiet, diagnostic way. Post-fight, Yoshibara looks at her hands for a moment, then nods. She respects what just happened.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Yoshibara doesn't double herself. She doubles Morida. He deflects his own double's attack for three seconds before he registers what he's deflecting. She hits him from behind during the pause. The commentary team has to explain this slowly. Masahata has to add a new row to the chart. Morida stands in the arena looking at his hands for a moment after the loss, then nods once.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Yoshibara advances to QF-2 vs Toragi (or Toshida if alt R1-2). Toragi's Twin Impact doesn't distinguish real from double — it detonates where it's placed. Toragi wins QF-2. SF-1 unchanged: Toragi advances. The Morida/Toragi post-fight conversation doesn't happen.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Morida watching Yoshibara lose to Toragi in QF-2 is a good moment. He sees exactly what Toragi did to beat the doubling. He writes it down. He writes Yoshibara's name next to it.

round_1.how_it_goes: Kitamura's wire network is up in four seconds — full arena coverage, tension-detection, every movement tracked. It's the same technique that eliminated Takazuki in the obstacle race. Chosen is not invisible and he is not trying to evade. He's been in three stressful situations this morning in front of thousands of people and the total is adding up. Round 2, Kitamura has to decide between holding the wire network and getting clear. The wire network is load-bearing for his entire strategy. He holds. Chosen walks through it. The wires register contact. Kitamura loses a tactically sound fight because the math stopped working.
round_1.dm_note: Kitamura's technique was correct — this should be acknowledged by the commentary. He didn't make a mistake. Chosen's escalation is the variable that doesn't fit the model. If Chosen is a PC, let the player feel the crowd noise and prior fights as an accumulated resource, not a liability — they're winning because of the stress, not despite it.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Chosen is at round 1 stress — this is only his first fight. The escalation hasn't built yet. The wire network maps him completely and every approach route is contested before he can gain momentum. Kitamura wins cleanly because the variable that breaks his strategy hasn't had time to accumulate. He notes privately that five more minutes would have been different.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Kitamura advances to QF-3 vs Kazama (or Takuma Rei if alt R1-1). Hellflame melts the wire network within two rounds — Kitamura had a contingency but not for sustained high-temperature discharge. Kazama wins QF-3 dramatically, wires catching fire in visible arcs. SF-2 changes completely: Kazama vs whoever won QF-4.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Chosen watching Kitamura in subsequent fights from the stands is worth playing. He's watching the wire technique specifically. He is cataloguing what he'd do with two more minutes of stress. He doesn't say this out loud.

round_1.how_it_goes: Yashiro teleports to a safe position. Ogiwara's first shockwave hits every point of the arena floor simultaneously. Yashiro teleports to a different safe position. The second shockwave informs him there are no safe positions. Yashiro stands still for a moment — genuinely thinking — and then raises his hand. The surrender takes less time than the audience expected. Ogiwara watches him leave with what appears to be mild disappointment that it ended there.
round_1.dm_note: The fight is quiet in a way that makes the shockwaves sound louder by contrast. Yashiro's pause before surrendering is important — he's not giving up, he's done the math. Play Ogiwara as someone who wanted a harder problem.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Yashiro reads the shockwave pulse pattern on the third wave and teleports not to a safe position but directly to Ogiwara — inside his personal space, too close for a shockwave to be aimed. Ogiwara didn't plan for close range. Neither did Yashiro, really. He wins on improvisation. He looks slightly bewildered by this.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Yashiro advances to QF-4 vs Kinosho (or Aikawa if alt R1-3). Kinosho deactivates the teleport. Yashiro appears mid-arc with his eyes wide, three feet from where he aimed, and Kinosho is already there. Kinosho wins. SF-2 unchanged: Kinosho advances.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Ogiwara watching Kinosho eliminate Yashiro in QF-4 is a quiet beat. He watches Kinosho work. He says 'I don't know what she did' about a completely different part of the fight than everyone expected. Masahata writes it down.

quarterfinals.note: Fighter B is whoever won R1-4 (default Izuto; Ando if alt R1-4; or Takazuki takes this slot if she upset Ren in R1-5, in which case fighter A becomes Takazuki)
quarterfinals.how_it_goes: The fastest fight of the tournament. Two speed-types. Ren is faster. Izuto knew this going in — he has been clocking Ren's movement since the entrance exam. He had a plan. The plan required three moves. He got through two. Ren wins. He looks at Izuto for a moment, says nothing. Izuto is already thinking about what he'd do with a third attempt.
quarterfinals.dm_note: If this is a PC fight, let Izuto's plan get close to working. The fun isn't Ren dominating — it's Izuto coming so close and still losing. The 'third attempt' feeling should be palpable.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Izuto's plan required three moves. He got through two last time — in every run-through in his head. This time he gets through all three. The third move accounts for the specific way Ren decelerates before contact. Izuto wins. He does not look pleased — he's already thinking about what a fourth move would have looked like. Ren stands at the edge of the arena for a moment, then leaves without saying anything.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Izuto advances to SF-1 vs Toragi (QF-2 default winner). Toragi's dodge-corridor trick worked on Ren because his dodge pattern was readable. Izuto's pattern is adaptive. She needs a different approach. She finds it — but it takes longer. Toragi wins SF-1, but the fight is her hardest of the tournament.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Post-fight: Ren finds Izuto. He says: 'The third move.' Izuto says: 'Yes.' That is the whole conversation. Run it exactly like the Ren/Toragi version — two sentences, mutual understanding, don't add to it.

quarterfinals.note: Fighter B is whoever won R1-2 (default Toragi; Toshida if alt R1-2). Fighter A is whoever won R1-6 (default Morida; Yoshibara if alt R1-6)
quarterfinals.how_it_goes: Best fight of the quarterfinals. Morida's quirk is close-range precision deflection — he's drilled for disrupting attack timing. Toragi's Twin Impact relies on delayed detonation: Morida disrupts the timing on the first Impact, breaking the delay mechanism. Nobody has done this to Toragi before. She adjusts. The second Impact lands. Toragi wins. Both of them are satisfied with the result in a way that's unusual for a tournament fight.
quarterfinals.dm_note: Post-fight: Morida approaches Toragi. He says: 'Your Impact timing — I disrupted it once. That's the only time that's worked on anyone I've spoken to.' She says: 'I know.' He nods. They both write this down. This is the beginning of a mutual-respect dynamic.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Toragi adjusts after the first Impact fails — but Morida disrupts the adjustment itself. He has drilled specifically for follow-through mechanics, not just the initial timing. The second Impact breaks cleanly too. Toragi runs out of Impact variants she has prepared. Morida wins the best fight of the quarterfinals. Both of them write this down.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Morida advances to SF-1 vs Ren (QF-1 default winner). Morida's deflection can redirect Ren's burst — but Ren is too fast for reliable deflection contact. Ren wins on the third exchange when Morida misreads the deceleration point. The Morida/Toragi post-fight conversation changes: he finds her anyway afterward. 'I disrupted both of them.' She says: 'I know.' Neither of them sounds happy about it.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: If Morida makes SF-1, the stakes of Ren vs Morida are higher than Ren vs Toragi for the 1-A/1-B rivalry dynamic. The class is watching a 1-B student reach the final. Masahata has updated the chart three times by this point.

quarterfinals.note: Fighter A is whoever won R1-7 (default Chosen; Kitamura if alt R1-7). Fighter B is whoever won R1-1 (default Kazama; Takuma Rei if alt R1-1)
quarterfinals.how_it_goes: Kazama's Hellflame escalates the ambient temperature and chaos — exactly the wrong environment for fighting Chosen Tadachi. Each fire blast makes Chosen more dangerous. The crowd is extremely loud about Hellflame and completely unprepared for what Stress looks like by round 4. Kazama loses a spectacular, close fight that the crowd talks about more than most of the wins.
quarterfinals.dm_note: First time the stadium sees Chosen at escalation. Run round 4 as genuinely alarming — Chosen should not feel comfortable to be near by the end. Kazama's loss should feel unfair in a way that's hard to articulate: he did everything right and it still made things worse.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Kazama's plan: end it before Chosen can build. He stops performing for the crowd entirely — the quietest Hellflame the stadium has ever seen. He fights like nobody's watching. Round 1 Chosen is manageable. Round 1 Chosen who hasn't escalated yet is winnable. Kazama wins before round 3. The crowd is slightly disappointed. Kazama looks more comfortable than he has all day.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Kazama advances to SF-2 vs Kinosho (QF-4 default winner). Kinosho deactivates Hellflame mid-charge. Kazama's physical technique without the quirk is still considerable — he trained before his quirk manifested. Kinosho wins after four rounds. Kazama finds this acceptable.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: The crowd's reaction to a quiet Kazama win is interesting — they wanted the spectacle and didn't get it. He hears this. He is not sure how to feel about having won by being less than what people came to see.

quarterfinals.note: Fighter A is whoever won R1-8 (default Ogiwara; Yashiro if alt R1-8). Fighter B is whoever won R1-3 (default Kinosho; Aikawa if alt R1-3)
quarterfinals.how_it_goes: Ogiwara's seismic shockwaves are structural — Kinosho's Neutralization can't deactivate something already in the ground. Her first plan fails visibly. Her second plan involves assembling something from arena debris during the fight. Ogiwara ends up briefly suspended in mid-air by a contraption Kinosho assembled in forty seconds. She does not have a construction quirk. Nobody asks how she did this. 'What Is She Doing?' airs for the second time.
quarterfinals.dm_note: Ogiwara's post-fight comment: 'I don't know what she did.' He has never said this about an opponent. He is not embarrassed — genuinely interested. Masahata writes this down immediately.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Kinosho's debris contraption works for thirty seconds. Ogiwara's second shockwave set dismantles it from underneath — she's building in a shaking building. She gets to the third component and the foundation isn't there. She concedes. Ogiwara says afterward: 'I don't know what she was building.' He says it like someone who genuinely wants to know.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Ogiwara advances to SF-2 vs Chosen (QF-3 default winner). Shockwaves plus crowd noise plus three prior fights feed Chosen's escalation. Chosen wins — the arena chaos helps him more than it hurts him. If Kazama won QF-3 instead: Ogiwara vs Kazama in SF-2. Seismic shockwaves destabilize Kazama's footing; Hellflame can't aim at something that's already moving the floor. Ogiwara wins.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: 'What Is She Doing?' never airs for the second time. The second segment was going to be about this fight. The silence where it would have been is its own thing.

semifinals.note: Fighter A is whoever won QF-1. Fighter B is whoever won QF-2. Default path is Ren vs Toragi.
semifinals.how_it_goes: UPSET. Twin Impact's first hit is a decoy — placed to create a specific dodge corridor. Ren dodges it exactly as Toragi predicted. The real Impact triggers on the dodge vector. Ren cannot outrun something that hits where he's going, not where he is. He loses a very fast fight in a way that shocks everyone including him. He stands at the edge of the arena for a moment after the loss, not saying anything, then leaves. He thinks about the second hit for the rest of the campaign.
semifinals.dm_note: Post-fight: Ren finds Toragi. He says: 'The second hit.' She says: 'Yes.' That is the whole conversation. Two sentences that communicate complete mutual understanding. Do not add to it.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Ren clocks that Toragi is placing Impacts along predicted dodge corridors. His solution: don't dodge. He takes the first Impact and wins through it. Nobody anticipated this. Toragi had no contingency for an opponent who wasn't trying to avoid it. Ren wins. He looks slightly surprised by his own decision.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Ren advances to the Final vs Chosen (SF-2 default winner). Speed vs escalation — Ren needs to end it by round 2 at the latest. If he does, it's the fastest final in the festival's history. If he doesn't, round 3 Chosen is a different problem entirely. The post-fight Ren/Toragi conversation still happens: 'The first hit.' She says: 'Yes.' Same two sentences. Different hit.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: A Ren vs Chosen final has a completely different energy than Toragi vs Chosen. Ren is fast enough to beat Chosen's escalation if he's disciplined. Whether he's disciplined is the question. The class watching this final knows the answer better than Ren does.

semifinals.note: Fighter A is whoever won QF-3. Fighter B is whoever won QF-4. Default path is Chosen vs Kinosho.
semifinals.how_it_goes: Kinosho targets Chosen's quirk with Neutralization. But Chosen's Stress isn't purely active — it's reactive. The crowd noise, the semifinal stakes, the previous three fights all feed the passive escalation that Kinosho can't fully suppress. She deactivates what she can. The ambient stress feeds what she can't. Chosen wins and is visibly running very hot walking out of the arena.
semifinals.dm_note: The crowd noise is a game mechanic here. Neutralization removes the quirk label but not the underlying stress response from competing in front of thousands of people. Chosen's win should feel slightly worrying, not triumphant.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Kinosho identifies that specific commentary vocal patterns correlate with Chosen's passive spikes. She suppresses active stimulation precisely — not reacting, not escalating the fight, giving him nothing to build on. The passive accumulation continues but slowly. She closes the window before it reaches critical. Kinosho wins. 'What Is She Doing?' — final segment — airs for the last time.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Kinosho advances to the Final vs Toragi (SF-1 default winner). Two methodical fighters. Kinosho neutralizes Twin Impact once. Toragi has been drilling her approach without the Impact since the Morida fight showed her it could be disrupted. She lands the approach clean. Both are fighting at their actual ceiling. Kinosho wins on round four when Toragi's timing becomes predictable. The crowd goes quiet in a way that signals they watched something exceptional but aren't sure how to celebrate it.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: A Kinosho vs Toragi final is the quietest possible ending for this tournament. Run it that way. No commentary on what's happening — just the two of them, and the audience slowly realising it was over before they understood what they were watching.

third_place.note: Fighters are the two SF losers. Default is Ren vs Kinosho.
third_place.how_it_goes: Ren wins. He says nothing about it afterward. Kinosho seems to find this appropriate. Masahata notes the result, looks at the ranking chart, and adds a column titled 'semantic outcomes.' Nobody asks him what this means.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Kinosho wins. Ren loses cleanly and accepts it cleanly. He nods once. She seems satisfied. Masahata adds 'semantic outcomes: confirmed' to the chart.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: Either result for the third-place match is fine. Run it based on what the players need from it emotionally. If Ren needs a win after a brutal SF loss, give him the 3rd place. If Kinosho needs to close her arc with something definitive, give her the win instead.

final.note: Fighters are the two SF winners. Default is Toragi vs Chosen.
final.favored: Toragi — technically superior, has defeated Class 1-B's seed 2, has a tested plan for Chosen's escalation curve
final.how_it_goes: Toragi has studied Chosen's last three fights. Her plan: end it before he escalates. She lands the first hit — a perfect technical strike, the cleanest move in the tournament. It is the worst decision she could have made. Chosen at round 1 stress level is manageable. Chosen after being struck in a national broadcast final, having already fought three escalating fights, is not. The fight drags into round 5. Nobody predicted round 5. The commentary team goes very quiet in round 4. It ends when Chosen's accumulated escalation exceeds anything Toragi's technique can counter. CHOSEN WINS. The commentary team is silent for four full seconds. Then very loud.
final.dm_note: Toragi's plan should be visibly correct until the moment it isn't. Her frustration should come from having done everything right. Chosen's win should feel slightly too big for the arena — not triumphant, but overwhelming. He comes down slowly. Give that moment: the quiet after the final ends, Chosen standing in the arena while the crowd is still loud.

alternate_outcome.how_it_goes: Toragi's plan was always correct. She identified round 2 as the window and she closes it. The first hit is perfect. She doesn't immediately go for the second — she waits, watches him start to build, then closes the window before it opens. She wins in round 2. Chosen walks out elevated. He sits with that for a long time.
alternate_outcome.downstream: Toragi wins the Sports Festival. The commentary team calls it 'flawless.' She watches the replay once and privately disagrees with the word. The class gets the quieter closing moment: Toragi in the arena, the crowd loud around her, already thinking about the next problem.
alternate_outcome.dm_note: A Toragi win is the technically correct outcome — it's what the data supported. Run her closing moment as satisfaction without triumph. She won because her preparation was better. That's enough. The Null Signal faction watching the broadcast takes specific interest in Toragi's precision: better footage of her technique than the exam drone captured.

post_tournament_moments: Morida finds Toragi after QF-2. 'Your Impact timing — I disrupted it once. That's the only time that's worked on anyone I've spoken to.' She says: 'I know.' He nods. They both write this down.
Ren finds Toragi after SF-1. 'The second hit.' She says: 'Yes.' That's the whole conversation. He thinks about it for the rest of the campaign.
Kazama's Hellflame drew the biggest crowd reaction of any fight. Someone sends him a photo of the crowd mid-blast. He never shows it to anyone.
'What Is She Doing?' about Kinosho airs twice. She watches both replays once in the waiting room, alone. She seems satisfied.
Ogiwara, after losing to Kinosho: 'I don't know what she did.' He has never said this about an opponent. Masahata writes this down immediately.
The ranking chart — brought to the festival laminated — does not get updated for four days. When asked why: 'The model is broken.' He reprints it anyway.

villain_seeds.all_factions: All three factions are watching the Sports Festival broadcast. Null Signal is collecting comprehensive quirk activation data — better footage than the exam drone captured. Fracture cells are watching because the festival represents exactly the system they oppose: licensed quirk use as public spectacle. Undertow watches Chosen's final with specific interest. None of this is visible to the class.

arcs.name: The Fracture Problem
arcs.dm_goal: Force the class into a confrontation they can't punch away. Shard should land at least two arguments the players can't easily dismiss. This is not a fight arc — it's a values arc. Null Signal is watching from the crowd periphery and the class probably doesn't notice.
arcs.description: Fracture stages a large public protest outside a Hero Commission building in central Murano. Unlicensed quirk users are demanding licensing reform, backed by Fracture cells providing protection. When the protest tips into a confrontation with Commission security, Class 1-A is deployed for crowd management. They meet Shard and Voltage directly. The situation is complicated by the fact that some protesters have genuine grievances, Fracture is genuinely protecting people in this moment, and Null Signal operatives are photographing quirk activation patterns from the crowd periphery.
beats: Arrival: the protest is peaceful, large, and legally ambiguous. Fracture's jagged-line symbol is everywhere. The students have seen it before — exam graffiti, dorm leaflet, alley concrete. It lands differently at scale.

Shard approaches the class directly, no aggression. She wants to debate. Her points are specific: the licensing system disadvantages people in communities like the port district. The Commission the class works for actively suppressed investigation into the Chain. Heroes who could have stopped the port district problem chose not to. 'I'm not asking you to agree with me. I'm asking you to explain why I'm wrong.' Give her at least two points that land with the players.

The escalation: Commission security physically moves an elderly protester who wasn't causing problems. Voltage intervenes — stops the guard with a paralytic shock, positions himself between the guard and the protester. This is a hero move performed by a villain. The class has to decide how to respond.

Null Signal in the margins: Splice is in the crowd, two rows back from the Commission building's west entrance. He's photographing the class — specifically their quirk activation patterns under stress. DC 18 Perception to notice him. If noticed, he's gone by the time anyone looks twice.

Resolution: Shard withdraws the Fracture cells before things become unmanageable. She does not run. She says: 'We'll be back when you've figured out which side you're on.' Voltage leaves last, making sure the elderly protester is clear before he goes.

villain_seeds.fracture: Shard and Voltage are fully visible — named, confronted, argued with. The class knows who they are now. What the class does with Shard's arguments is the open question they carry into the finale.
villain_seeds.null_signal: Splice's photographic operation in the crowd. Null Signal now has comprehensive quirk-activation footage of every PC under stress conditions. Whether the class noticed him determines how much warning they have.
villain_seeds.the_chain: Shard mentions once that the Commission ignored the Chain's port district operation for eighteen months. She has documentation. She doesn't share it today. She knows the class was at Patchwork Agency — she has sources in the neighbourhood.

arcs.name: Convergence
arcs.dm_goal: All three factions in the same space. The class wins the immediate fight but walks out with Undertow at large, Null Signal's data collected, and no clean answer on Fracture. This is a victory that opens more questions than it closes.
arcs.description: Acting on information from Oshiro (sourced through Patchwork's contacts), the class intercepts a Chain logistics operation in the port district warehouse district. Kuma is running it. What they don't know: Null Signal's Splice has identified this operation as a data acquisition target — the Chain is transporting experimental quirk-suppression equipment, and Null Signal wants it. Fracture is also here, sabotaging Chain equipment because the Chain extorts the unlicensed quirk users in their community. The class walks into three factions with three different agendas and has to make decisions in real time.
beats: Arrival: the warehouse district at night. Signs of the Chain operation — logistics vehicles, a perimeter. Signs of something else — two Fracture cell members are already inside, cutting equipment cables. Signs of something else entirely — a Null Signal technical van is parked across the street monitoring equipment frequencies. The class has thirty seconds before Kuma notices the Fracture interference.

Kuma: loud, overconfident, not in on the Null Signal or Fracture presence. 'You're graduating early, students.' He starts Large. He reaches Huge by round 2. His growth is capped at Huge — the warehouse ceiling prevents Gargantuan. Use this. The class should figure out that the architecture is their advantage.

Splice: enters from the north loading door in round 3, after assessing that the fight will compromise the equipment he came for. He does not engage the class unless they block his path to the crates. If the class is occupied with Kuma, he takes what he came for and leaves clean. Either way, Null Signal gets something.

Shard: she's on the roof of the adjacent building, watching. If the class gets into serious trouble, she could intervene — whether she does depends on how they handled The Fracture Problem. She will not fight the class tonight regardless.

Undertow: he phases through the warehouse's east wall in the final round. He stands at the edge of the fight. He watches Kuma go down. He watches Splice complete or abandon his objective. He looks at each PC for three seconds each — unhurried, interested. Then he phases through the floor. He says one thing as he goes: 'Good instincts. Wrong scale.' He is gone.

Resolution: Kuma is arrested and in custody. Splice has retreated. Shard is gone. Undertow was never touchable. The class has won the immediate fight. Oshiro arrives — without being called. He reads a single-line report. He puts it down. He says: 'Get some sleep.' The report heading, visible to a sharp-eyed player: 'Undertow — confirmed active, Murano port. Status: at large.'

season_1_hooks.undertow: At large. 'Wrong scale' is not a dismissal — it's an assessment. He's decided the class is interesting. Season 2 opens with Undertow being specifically interested in Class 1-A.
season_1_hooks.null_signal: Whether Splice got the equipment or not, Null Signal has comprehensive combat data on every PC's quirk activation. Director Aoi Tenshin (Null) has been briefed. Her log entry describes the class as 'worth acquiring.' In Null Signal's context, this means something specific.
season_1_hooks.fracture: Shard's question — 'which side are you on?' — is unanswered. The class's relationship with Fracture in Season 2 depends entirely on how they handled The Fracture Problem.
season_1_hooks.flashpoint_tease: Among the Chain's seized equipment: an internal memo referencing 'Miruki's operation, port district, pending approval.' The name means nothing to the class yet.
season_1_hooks.riddle_tease: One Chain cell member being processed says: 'We have a friend who talks to people. He talked to someone you know.' He won't say more. Plant this ambiguously — it could be Riddle, or it could be a bluff.
season_1_hooks.null_tease: Splice's retreating van. A plate number and a logo: 'Seika Advanced Systems.' The same name from Hamagawa's monitor screen in Tier 1, if anyone remembered it.

villain_seeds.all_factions: All three factions are fully present and active. The Chain (Kuma) is the direct confrontation — arrested. Null Signal (Splice) achieves a partial objective regardless of what the class does. Fracture (Shard) is neither defeated nor allied. Undertow's observation and departure is the closing image of Season 1.

tiers.villain_presence: All three factions are fully active in Tier 2. The Chain (Kuma) is the direct confrontation — a winnable boss fight. Null Signal (Splice) is the operationally sophisticated secondary threat who achieves something regardless of how the main fight goes. Fracture (Shard, Voltage) is the moral complication that doesn't resolve cleanly. Undertow's Season 1 closing appearance — watching, commenting, leaving untouched — establishes the scale of the problem the class is not yet equipped to address.

recurring_elements.ranking_chart: Masahata's ranking chart on the common room fridge persists through the entire campaign. Reference it at least once per tier. By Season 1's end it has been annotated, defaced, coffee-stained, and re-laminated over the original lamination. He reprints it every time. This is never commented on directly.
recurring_elements.oshiro_arrivals: Whenever things get genuinely serious, Oshiro Daikichi arrives at the scene without being called. No explanation is offered. He just shows up. This should remain unexplained through Season 1.
recurring_elements.kinosho_napping: Kinosho Kochimiko is asleep somewhere in the background of every session where she is not the primary focus. She is always awake again before it matters.
recurring_elements.toshida_head: Toshida's speech bubble head gets stuck in, on, or through something structural at least once per tier. The scale escalates as his quirk control improves but his spatial awareness does not.
recurring_elements.faction_symbol_thread: The Fracture jagged-line symbol appears in each Tier 1 arc (entrance exam wall, dorm leaflet, alley concrete). By the time Shard introduces herself in The Fracture Problem, the class has seen it three times in contexts they didn't understand. The connection is available in retrospect.
